coredata.dc:title: A label-free nano-probe for sequential and quantitative determination of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid in real samples based on S and N dual-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Shengmei
Liang, Fan
Li, Minglu
Du, Fangfang
Dong, Wenjuan
Gong, Xiaojuan
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent sulfur and nitrogen dual-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) was prepared by a simple and one-step acid-base neutralization and exothermic carbonization method. Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) could effectively quench the fluorescence of S,N-CDs based on inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic quenching, whereas ascorbic acid (AA) could recover the fluorescence of S,N-CDs/Cr(VI) because of IFE weakening. So an “on-off-on” and label-free nano-probe consecutive determination of Cr(VI) and AA was constructed. This nano-probe system demonstrated excellent selectivity and sensitivity to Cr(VI) and AA with linear range of 0.065–198 μmol/L (3.38–10,296 μg/L) and 6.6–892 μmol/L (1.16–157 mg/L), respectively. Meanwhile, the as-prepared S,N-CDs possess low toxicity and could be used for multi-color cell imaging in SMMC 7721 cells. More importantly, this nano-probe was successfully employed for detection of Cr(VI) in tap water and AA in food samples. In view of its simple detection condition, rapid response, wide linear range, low detection limit and inexpensive instrument, the as-constructed nano-probe system could have a wide range of potential application, including water quality monitoring and evaluation, food inspection and testing and biomedical analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S,N dual-doped carbon dots
Fluorescent nano-probe
Cr(VI)
Ascorbic acid
Real samples

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: A label-free nano-probe for Cr(VI) detection
The nano-probe for determination of AA
MTT assays and cellular imaging of S,N-CDs
Detection of Cr(VI) in tap water and AA in food amples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption (black line), fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of S,N-CDs in aqueous solution (0.22 mg/mL). Inset: photographs of S,N-CDs solution under visible light (left) and UV light irradiation at 365 nm (right). (B) The emission spectra of S,N-CDs obtained under different excitation wavelengths.

(A) N1 s, (B) C1s and (C) S2p XPS spectrum of the as-prepared S,N-CDs. (D) TEM, (E) AFM and (F) DLS images of the as-fabricated S,N-CDs. The insets in (D) are size distribution of S,N-CDs (left bottom) and HRTEM image of S,N-CDs with lattice parameters of 0.22 nm (top right).

(A) The ion selectivity of S,N-CDs. Comparison of fluorescence intensity of 0.22 mg/mL S,N-CDs after the addition of 3.2 mmol/L other different metal ions. (B) Comparison of fluorescence intensity of 0.22 mg/mL S,N-CDs after the addition of 6.2 mmol/L other different metal ions and 0.62 mmol/L Cr(VI). (C) The fluorescence spectra of 0.22 mg/mL S,N-CDs in the absence or presence of Cr(VI). The concentration of Cr(VI) (1–26) are: 0, 0.065, 2, 5.3, 10, 15, 26.4, 32.9, 39.3, 45.8, 61.9, 71.5, 81.2, 90.8, 97.2, 110, 119.5, 129, 141.7, 154, 170, 183, 198, 220, 242 and 273 μmol/L, respectively. Inset: Dependence of F0/F on the various concentrations of Cr(VI) (0.065–273 μmol/L) with the Ex and Em are 372 nm and 465 nm, and the excitation and emission slits are 10 nm and 10 nm, respectively. (D) The absorbance spectra titration of Cr(VI) at 0.22 mg/mL S,N-CDs in ultrapure water. The concentration of Cr(VI) (1–16) are: 0, 30, 60, 90,120, 150,180, 210, 270, 300, 330, 390, 420, 450, 480 and 510 μmol/L respectively.

(A) Comparison of fluorescence intensity of 0.22 mg/mL S,N-CDs and 0.65 mmol/L Cr(VI) after the addition of different reducing agents (2.3 mmol/L). (B) The fluorescence spectra of 0.22 mg/mL S,N-CDs and 0.60 mmol/L Cr(VI) in the absence or presence of AA. The concentration of AA (1–18) are: 0, 6.6, 13.2, 26.3, 75.8, 158, 240, 322, 404, 485, 567, 648, 730, 810, 892, 988, 1117 and 1246 μmol/L, respectively. Inset: Dependence of F0 F on the AA concentrations (6.6–1246 μmol/L). Ex = 372 nm, Em = 465 nm, and the excitation and emission slits are 10 nm and 10 nm, respectively. (C) The absorbance spectra titration of AA at 0.32 mg/mL S,N-CDs and 0.51 mmol/L Cr(VI) in ultrapure water. The concentration of AA (1–14) are: 0, 0.066, 0.16, 0.26, 0.36, 0.36, 0.56, 0.66, 0.76, 0.85, 0.95, 0.10 and 0.11 mmol/L, respectively.

(A) Cell viability test of S,N-CDs on SMMC 7721 cells incubated with different concentrations of S,N-CDs at 37 °C for 48 h. The values represent percentage cell viability (mean % ± SD, n = 5). (B) The LSCM images of SMMC 7721 cells incubated with 250 μg/mL S,N-CDs at 37 °C for 2 h. B1, B2, B3 and B4 are cell images taken at λex/λem of 543/650 ± 25, 488/500 ± 25, 405/422 ± 25 and 515/570 ± 25 nm, respectively, B5 shows the bright field image of SMMC 7721 cells, and B6 is the merged images of B1, B2, B3, B4 and B5.

The effect on the fluorescence intensity of LSCM by S,N-CDs, Cr(VI) and AA. (A) The LSCM images of SMMC 7721 cells incubated with S,N-CDs at 37 °C for 4 h. (B) The LSCM images of SMMC 7721 cells incubated with 250 μg/mL S,N-CDs and Cr(VI) at 37 °C for 20 min. The LSCM dynamic scanning of SMMC 7721 cells incubated with 400 μg/mL S,N-CDs at 37 °C for 4 h, adding 5 μmol/L Cr(VI) (C) to observe the fluorescence quenching, and then adding 290 μmol/L AA (D) to observe fluorescence recovery, and the scan interval is 60 s. λex/λem = 488/500 ± 25.
The analytical features comparison of CDs in some literatures and the proposed employed for Cr(VI) detection.
Cr(VI) detection in tap water samples.

head.ce:title: A label-free nano-probe for sequential and quantitative determination of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid in real samples based on S and N dual-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent sulfur and nitrogen dual-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) was prepared by a simple and one-step acid-base neutralization and exothermic carbonization method. Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) could effectively quench the fluorescence of S,N-CDs based on inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic quenching, whereas ascorbic acid (AA) could recover the fluorescence of S,N-CDs/Cr(VI) because of IFE weakening. So an “on-off-on” and label-free nano-probe consecutive determination of Cr(VI) and AA was constructed. This nano-probe system demonstrated excellent selectivity and sensitivity to Cr(VI) and AA with linear range of 0.065–198 μmol/L (3.38–10,296 μg/L) and 6.6–892 μmol/L (1.16–157 mg/L), respectively. Meanwhile, the as-prepared S,N-CDs possess low toxicity and could be used for multi-color cell imaging in SMMC 7721 cells. More importantly, this nano-probe was successfully employed for detection of Cr(VI) in tap water and AA in food samples. In view of its simple detection condition, rapid response, wide linear range, low detection limit and inexpensive instrument, the as-constructed nano-probe system could have a wide range of potential application, including water quality monitoring and evaluation, food inspection and testing and biomedical analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of carbon-based zero-dimensional nanomaterial, which was discovered by Xu et al. in 2004 [1]. CDs possess many superior features, such as excellent optical properties, good water solubility, low toxicity, inexpensive and favorable biocompatibility [2–4]. Based on these advantages, CDs are widely used in many fields such as cellular imaging technology [5–8], environmental monitoring [9–14], drug delivery, target-imaging, diagnosis and therapy of cancer [15–18], etc. CDs are mainly composed of C, H and O, which possess non-perfect sp2 domains [19]. S and N dual-doped CDs (S,N-CDs) have received much attention because the electronegativity of S atom is similar to that of the C atom, and the atomic radius of N atom is similar to C atom. And the study indicated that the CO group on the surface of CDs and the CN and CN bonds in the carbon nucleus are respectively the main luminescence center of blue fluorescence and the key factors to increase the luminescence intensity. Meanwhile, S has a synergistic effect on the luminescence properties of N-CDs [20,21].

Chromium compounds widely exist in many industries such as welding, metal finishing, leather tanning, etc. [22,23]. Chromium (Cr) in the environment is primarily in two valence states, trivalent Cr (Cr(III)) and hexavalent Cr (Cr(VI)). The reports pointed out that Cr(VI) can cause respiratory [24] and digestive diseases, anemia and cancer to humans [25]. Cr(VI) usually occurs as Cr2O7 2−, which possess highly soluble and toxic [26]. EPA is reviewing health effects data of Cr(VI) and may set limits on its levels in drinking water in the future. Therefore, the determination of trace Cr(VI) is very important. There are many analytical methods were used for the determination of Cr at trace levels, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [27–30], atomic emission spectrometry [31,32], ion chromatography [33–35], mass spectrometry [27,36,37] and X-ray fluorescence [38–40]. These analytical methods with some defects, for example, indistinguishable Cr(VI) and Cr(III), expensive instrument or complicated analysis process. In view of the substantial differences in the biological availability and the toxicity of Cr(VI) compared with Cr(III) (the essential nutrient for human, in amounts of 50–200 μg/day), differential analysis for Cr species is of great scientific and public health concern. However, few analytical data differentiate between these two oxidation states [24–26]. In recent years, nano-sensing systems have been used to determine trace Cr(VI), such as graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets [41], graphene oxide modified with amino silanes [42], gold nanoparticles [43,44], fluorescent iridium nanoclusters [45], PbS quantum dot [46] and CDs [47,48]. So, it is necessary to develop a simple and rapid method for Cr(VI) detection in routine analysis.

Ascorbic acid (AA), a water-soluble and natural organic compound with antioxidant properties, is an important vitamin widely existed in fresh fruits, vegetables and many organisms [49,50]. As a highly active substance, it participates in many metabolic processes [51,52]. In recent years, AA has aroused people's interest as one of the members which protect from free radical damage in vivo such as anti-aging [53,54], resuscitation after severe burn injury [55], anticancer [56] and other diseases [57,58]. Therefore, its content can be used as an important physiological index of anti-aging and anti-adversity, and it is also of great significance in distinguishing the quality of fruit trees and breeding good varieties. At present, many methods for AA determination have been reported, such as capillary zone electrophoresis and micellar electrokinetic chromatography [59], electrochemical nanosensor [60–63], HPLC [64], electrochemical method [65,66], Rayleigh scattering [67] and CDs nanosensor [52,68,69]. However, more selective, sensitive and accurate analytical methods for AA determination are still in urgent demand.

In this paper, S,N-CDs were fabricated by simply mixing of glucose, concentrated H2SO4 and EDA, which served as C, S and N source, respectively. Based on the fluorescence of S,N-CDs could be significantly quenched by Cr(VI) and recovered by AA partially (about 73%), the fluorescence nano-probe for the detection of Cr(VI) and AA was constructed. And the fluorescence of S,N-CDs quenched by Cr(VI) is attributed to the dynamic quenching and IFE. AA reduces Cr(VI) to Cr3+, thereby weakening the IFE and restoring the fluorescence of S,N-CDs. Compared with the previous Cr(VI) detection methods [28,29,31–40], the proposed method is more advantageous, such as simple detection condition, rapid response, wide linear range, low detection limit and inexpensive instrument. This nano-probe displayed outstanding selectivity and sensitivity to Cr(VI) and AA with the linearity range of 0.065–198 μmol/L (3.38–10,296 μg/L) and 6.6–892 μmol/L (1.16–157 mg/L), respectively. S,N-CDs possess low cytotoxicity and excellent cytocompatibility, which could exhibit red, green and blue emissions in SMMC 7721 cells on Zeiss LSM880 confocal laser scanning microscope, illustrating that the S,N-CDs is an eminent reagent for cellular imaging.

Glucose, concentration H2SO4, 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) and other chemical reagents were obtained from Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), 19 kinds of metal ions (K+, Na+, Pb2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Al3+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr(VI), Cu2+, Ba2+, Zn2+ and Mn(VII)), 13 kinds of anion (I−, S2−, NO3 −, C2O4 2−, CO3 2−, Br−, F−, Cl−, S2O3 2−, SO3 2−, SCN−, H2PO4 − and HPO4 2−), 18 kinds of amino acids (Ala, Leu, Ile, Trp, Gly, Ser, Cys, Tyr, His, Lys, Glu, Asn, Gln, Val, Phe, Thr, Arg and Asp) and 9 kinds of anion reducing agents (KBH4, ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid, glucose, glutathione (GSH), Glycine (Gly), Cysteine (Cys) and glutamic acid (Glu)) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All reagents are analytical purity and without further purification before used. Aqueous solution applied in all experiments was prepared using ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ·cm) from a Milli-Q Plus System (Millipore, USA).

S,N-CDs were fabricated by acid-base neutralization and exothermic carbonization method of 0.4 g glucose, 70% concentrated H2SO4 (4.0 mL) and 6.0 mL EDA, which used as C source, S dopant and N dopant, respectively. The solid was dissolved in ultrapure water, and then the supernatant was obtained after filtration. Finally, the supernatant was lyophilized to obtain the yellow powder after dialyzed for 72 h by dialysis membrane (1000 Da) and the water was renewed every 12 h.

The absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded on Lambda 365 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, UK) and Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA), respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and HR-TEM images were acquired on a field emission transmission electron microscope JEM-2100F (Japan Electronics Corporation, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were performed on Bruker AXS MultiMode® 8 (Bruker, Germany). The sample was obtained by dropping the appropriate concentration S,N-CDs solution on the newly-sliced mica sheet and dried at room temperature. Hydrodynamic size and size distribution of the S,N-CDs was determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern, UK). IR spectra were conducted on Bruker Tensor II Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) by the KBr tablet method. The content of elements and the chemical composition of the S,N-CDs were characterized by Thermo Escalab 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Elementar Vario Micro Cube (Elementar, Germany) was applied to analyze the composition of the S,N-CDs. The fluorescence life time was measured by an FLS 920 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., U K). Cell imaging was performed on a Zeiss LSM880 confocal laser scanning microscope (Zeiss, Germany). Sample preparation methods reference our previous article [5,9,10,15,19].

In order to determine the selectivity of S,N-CDs, 3 mL of ultrapure water was mingled with 70 μL 10 mg/mL S,N-CDs, and the fluorescence intensities were measured. Then 19 kinds of metal ions (100 μL, 0.1 mol/L), 13 kinds of anions (70 μL, 0.1 mol/L) and 18 kinds of amino acids (70 μL, 0.01 mol/L) were mixed with S,N-CDs, and then the fluorescence intensity was measured, respectively. The interferences of different metal ions were evaluated by mingling 3 mL of ultrapure water with 70 μL of 10 mg/mL S,N-CDs and measuring the fluorescence intensities. Then 70 μL of 0.1 mol/L metal ions were added into the mixture solution and well mixed, the fluorescence intensity was measured. Subsequently, 30 μL of 0.01 mol/L Cr(VI) was added, and then the fluorescence intensities were measured. In order to investigate the quenching effect of pH value, phosphate buffer solutions (PBS, 20 mmol/L) with different pH values were prepared, and the fluorescence quenching degree of S,N-CDs by Cr(VI) was determined by addition 70 μL of 10 mg/mL S,N-CDs in 3 mL of 20 mmol/L PBS and mixing with 5 μL of 0.1 mol/L Cr(VI), fluorescence intensity of the system was measured at each pH value. For the determination of Cr(VI), 3 mL of ultrapure water was mingled with 70 μL of 10 mg/mL S,N-CDs, and the fluorescence intensities were measured. Then series of appropriate volumes of 0.01 mol/L Cr(VI) were added, and the fluorescence intensities were measured after shaking and mixing for 3 min.

In a typical procedure, 3 mL of ultrapure water was mingled with 70 μL of 10 mg/mL S,N-CDs. Then 15 μL of 0.1 mol/L Cr(VI) was added in order to form the nano-probe (S,N-CDs/Cr(VI)), and now the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs is quenched to a minimum by the Cr(VI). Then the fluorescence intensity was recorded after adding different amounts of AA after shaking and mixing thoroughly at room temperature for 3 min to determine the concentration of AA.

The selectivity of the S,N-CDs/Cr(VI) nano-probe to AA was verified by addition other reducing agents (Glycine, Na2S, NaNO3, Na2SO3, Glutamic acid, KI, KBr, Na2S2O3, glucose, GSH, Citric acid and NaBH4) instead of AA in a similar way. For the fluorescence detection of AA in actual samples, 10 mL of a sample solution was mixed into 2 mL nano-probe (S,N-CDs/Cr(VI)) solution thoroughly at room temperature.
All the samples were detected fluorescence at λex = 372 nm, and the emissions were recorded at 390–600 nm.
Human hepatocarcinoma SMMC 7721 cell was employed to verify the cytotoxicity of S,N-CDs, and the experimental methods referred our published papers [5,9,10,15,19,70,71].

SMMC 7721 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), and the cells were seeded in glass bottom culture dishes at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere for 10 h. Then the cells are treated with S,N-CDs (100, 200, 250, 300 and 400 μg/mL) for 2 h at 37 °C after the removal of the DMEM. Subsequently, the cells were cleaned three times by pH 7.4 PBS to eliminate extracellular S,N-CDs and maintained in PBS for fluorescence imaging by a Zeiss LSM880 confocal laser scanning microscope. Then a concentration of 5.0 μmol/L of Cr(VI) was added, fluorescence images were collected with an interval of 60 s. Finally, the fluorescence images were collected with an interval of 60 s after addition 290 μmol/L AA.

All fresh fruits were obtained from a local market in Taiyuan and measured immediately after pretreatment. Fresh fruits and vegetables (apple, strawberry, tomato, jujube, red pepper and red pepper) samples were obtained as follows. Firstly, the fruits were cleaned with ultrapure water and weighed accurately after natural drying at room temperature. Then they were smashed by a small crusher. The power was dissolved in ultrapure water and centrifuged at 10 °C by a refrigerated centrifuge at 6000 rpm for 6.0 min. The supernatant was obtained by filtration centrifugal fluid through a 0.45 mm pore size filter membrane. Finally, the detection was executed after dilution the supernatant to an appropriate concentration by ultrapure water.

The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the S,N-CDs were explored and the results are displayed in Fig. 1A. The absorption spectra (black line) in Fig. 1A, the peaks at 280 and 333 nm are respectively due to π → π ⁎ transition of the CC bond and n → π ⁎ transition of the CO bond [72]. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of S,N-CDs were showed in Fig. 1A (red and blue lines), which demonstrates that the fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths of S,N-CDs are 372 nm and 465 nm, respectively. The S,N-CDs aqueous solution is yellow transparent in visible light and exhibits blue fluorescence in UV (365 nm) light (the inset in Fig. 1A). The fluorescence behavior was investigated to further verify the optical properties of the as-prepared S,N-CDs and the result was illustrated in Fig. 1B. When λex moves from 280 to 440 nm, the λem of S,N-CDs are bathochromic-shift from 468 to 495 nm, and in the meantime, the fluorescence intensity increases to the maximum at 380 nm and then decreases to the minimum at 495 nm. The excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of S,N-CDs is as a result of the surface state which affects the band gap of S,N-CDs [9,15]. Furthermore, the five-point method (show in supporting information) was used to calculate the quantum yield (QY), as shown in Table S1 and Fig. S1, the QY of the as-prepared S,N-CDs is concluded to be 5.1% [10,73].

The elemental analysis, FTIR and XPS were employed to inspect the surface states and chemical composition of the S,N-CDs. As shown in Table S2A, the as-constructed S,N-CDs is composed of C 16.66, H 6.38, S 19.21, N 17.61 and O (calculated) 39.99 wt%, which reveals that it is co-doped with sulfur and nitrogen. Table S2B manifests the relative atoms number according to the elemental contents. The empirical formula for an S,N-CD is maybe C2H11SN2O4. The XPS scan of S,N-CDs (Fig. S2A) exhibits five peaks at 527, 396, 280, 227 and 164 eV, which should be attributed to O1s, N1 s, C1s, S2 s and S2p, respectively. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S2B) was disintegrated into five peaks at 531.78, 531.17, 530.66, 530.17 and 529.66 eV, which are corresponding to pyridinic-like O/S-O, C-OH/C-O, C-O-C, O=C-O, and SO4 2− [5,9,15,19,74]. The N1 s spectrum of S,N-CDs in Fig. 2A depicts four peaks at 400.70, 399.86, 398.92 and 398.08 eV, corresponding to the form of pyridine nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen, such as pyridinic-like N, -NH2/N-OH and –CN/N-(C)N-(C), respectively [5,9,15,19,74]. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of the S,N-CDs (Fig. 2B) displays five peaks at 286.5, 285.5, 285.0, 284.5 and 283.5 eV, which are attributed to the C-O/C=O, C-N/C-O-C, C-C/C-H, CC and CS groups, respectively [5,9,15,19]. The S2p spectrum (Fig. 2C) presented three peaks at 168.65, 167.71 and 166.78, indicating that sulfur is present mainly in the form of a CS bond of thiophene sulfur and -C-SOx (x = 2, 3, 4, sulfate or sulfonate), and the peaks are attributed to the SO4 2−, N-S(O)/SO3 2− and N-S(O)/S2O3 2− groups, respectively [74]. Therefore, all the results demonstrate that the as-prepared S,N-CDs has been successfully doped by S and N.

The FTIR spectrum was also employed to survey the surface functional groups of the as-prepared S,N-CDs (Fig. S2C). A broad peak centered at 3462 cm−1 evidences the existence of OH or NH bonds stretching vibration [75]. The shoulder peak at 1381 and 3022 cm−1 should be belonged to the stretching vibrations of NH in amide bond and CH in a double bond, respectively. The peaks at 2930 and 2863 cm−1 are ascribed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of methylene and methyl. The three peaks at 2595, 2476 and 2366 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of NH in amine salts. The peaks at 2130 and 2180 cm−1 represent the stretching vibration of a triple bond or a cumulative double bond, such as C=C=O or CN. The peak at 1644 cm−1 denotes the stretching vibration of CO in an amide bond. And the peak at 1540 cm−1 expresses the in-plane bending of NH in secondary amides. The peaks at 1456 and 1385 cm−1 signify the asymmetric and symmetric bending vibrations of methylene and methyl, respectively. The peaks at 1343 and 1092 cm−1 could be belonged to the in-plane bending of OH and C-O/C-N, respectively. The peak at 975 cm−1 is the in-plane bending of CH in = C-H. And the peaks at 644 and 606 cm−1 originate from SO3 2−/SO4 2− [4,9,15]. The results of surface functional group recognition by FTIR are consistent with XPS results.

The morphology and size distribution of the as-prepared S,N-CDs was determined by TEM. The representative TEM image in Fig. 2D illustrates that the S,N-CDs are of mainly round and evenly distributed on the surface of the copper mesh. The particle size distribution of S,N-CDs is 2.2–5.8 nm, and the mean particle diameter is 3.4 ± 0.5 nm which is evaluated using 100 counts of the particles. The high-resolution TEM in Fig. 2D with distinct lattice fringes of 0.22 nm confirmed that the S,N-CDs possess crystal structure, which is similar to the 100 facet of graphitic structure [72]. The AFM image in Fig. 2E was used to further characterize the morphology of S,N-CDs, which confirmed that the particles of S,N-CDs are evenly distributed and the mean height is 0.5–2.5 nm. The DLS result in Fig. 2F depicts that the average hydrodynamic particle size of S,N-CDs is 9.6 ± 0.6 nm, indicating that the S,N-CDs is highly dispersible without any aggregation in aqueous solution. This result is consistent with the particle size distribution by TEM, whereas DLS estimates the particle size of S,N-CDs surrounded by water molecules.

pH value may affect the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared S,N-CDs, which was studied in detail. Fig. S3A indicates that the fluorescence intensities of the S,N-CDs at the concentration of 0.32 mg/mL are essentially stable in the pH range of 3.0–10.0, displaying the potential application of S,N-CDs in a wide pH range. As described in Fig. S3B, there is no visible change of fluorescence intensity after xenon arc light exposure for 180 min, which suggests the favorable photostability of S,N-CDs. The outstanding pH and photo-stability endow S,N-CDs with a wide range of applications, such as fluorescence nano-probe, environmental water quality monitoring and other biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A label-free nano-probe for Cr(VI) detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the prepared S,N-CDs, the effects on S,N-CDs fluorescence of 19 metal ions, 13 anions and 18 amino acids were investigated, and the results were illustrated in Fig. 3A, Figs. S4A and S4B, respectively. Fig. 3A enunciates that Fe3+ and Cr(VI) can lead to the noticeable quenching of the fluorescence intensity of S,N-CDs, whereas the other metal ions, anions (Fig. S4A) and amino acids (Fig. S4B) show a negligible quenching effect to the fluorescence of the S,N-CDs. Meanwhile, the anti-interference performance of the S,N-CDs/Cr(VI) system was also discussed, and some coexisting metal ions (Mn2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+ and Cr3+) at a 10-fold concentration of Cr(VI) lead to insignificant fluorescence change (Fig. 3B). It is found that Cr(VI) is much more responsive than Fe3+, which illustrates that the prepared S,N-CDs are highly more selective to Cr(VI) than the other metal ions. So it may be employed to determine Cr(VI).

As shown in Fig. S4C, the fluorescence quenching of S,N-CDs by Cr(VI) at different pH values was investigated. It depicts that the degree of fluorescence quenching increases with the pH increasing. The fluorescence quenching efficiency is 29% and 16% at pH 6.0 and 7.0, respectively. The fluorescence quenching efficiency is almost constant in the range of pH 7.0–12.0. The stable forms of Cr(VI) are almost always bound to oxygen (e.g., CrO4 2− and Cr2O7 2−). The two kinds of Cr(VI) participate in acid-base equilibrium as follows (25 °C) [76]:
(1)
HCrO 4
−
⇋
CrO 4
2
+
H +
,
K
a 2
= 3.2 ×
10
− 7
(2)
2
HCrO 4
−
⇋
Cr 2
O 7
2 −
+ 2
H 2
O ,
K = 33

According to Eqs. (1) and (2), Eq. (3) is obtained:
(3)
2
CrO 4
2 −
+ 2
H +
⇋
Cr 2
O 7
2 −
+ 2
H 2
O
K = 3.2 ×
10 14

According to the formula (3), the main existence of Cr2O7 2- when the solution is acidity (pH < 4.0), whereas CrO4 2− is the dominant ion when the solution is alkaline (pH > 10.0). Two kinds of ions coexist at pH = 7.0, and [Cr2O7 2−]/[CrO4 2−]2 = 3. Meanwhile, the solubility constant of Fe(OH)3 is 4 × 10−38 (25 °C) [77], and at pH = 7.0, [Fe3+] = 4 × 10−17. So Fe3+ does not interfere with the determination of Cr(VI) at pH = 7.0. In combination with Fig. S3A, the fluorescence intensity is also relatively stable at pH = 7.0. The determination of Cr(VI) in PBS would enhance the sensitivity, but some ions (e.g., Sn2+ and Pb2+) will interference the Cr(VI) determination by forming the precipitation. So in the subsequent experiments, ultrapure water was selected as the solvent to study the sensitivity and selectivity of the S,N-CDs and its application in actual samples evaluation.

Fluorescence titration experiments were used to investigate the Cr(VI) concentration dependence of S,N-CDs as shown in Fig. 3C. Fig. 3C shows that the fluorescence intensity of the S,N-CDs decreases with the addition of Cr(VI). The insets exhibit an excellent linearity for Cr(VI) in the range of 0.065–198 μmol/L (i.e.: 3.38–10,296 μg/L) with a linear equation of y = 0.0052× + 0.9817 (R2 = 0.9957, n = 23). And Ksv is calculated to be 5.2 × 103 L/mol. Here F and F0 are the fluorescent intensities of S,N-CDs at 465 nm in the presence and absence of Cr(VI). The limit of detection (LOD) of the nano-probe was estimated to be 0.56 nmol/L based on three times the standard deviation divided by K
sv [19]. SASO, GSO, and WHO proposes that the limit concentration of Cr in drinking water is 50 μg/L, implying that the constructed S,N-CDs nano-probe is excellent to determine the content of Cr(VI) in tap water. Furthermore, the analytical features comparison of some literatures [10,47,48,68,69,78] and the proposed S,N-CDs employed for Cr(VI) detection is listed in Table 1 , which could be found that the proposed method possess a wide linear range, low detection limit and simple operating conditions.

Absorption spectra titration experiments were carried out to explain the interaction mechanism between S,N-CDs and Cr(VI) as shown in Fig. 3D. When Cr(VI) was added into S,N-CDs solution, the characteristic absorption peaks of Cr(VI) at 258, 350 and 446 nm were emerged and increased with the increasing of the Cr(VI) concentration, and no offset of the peak position and no new peaks were observed, demonstrating no new substance was generated. Fig. 3C illustrates that the fluorescence intensity at 465 nm of the S,N-CDs decreases with the increasing of Cr(VI), and this phenomenon may be caused by two reasons. Firstly, a stronger absorption peak of Cr(VI) at 350 nm absorbed the excitation energy of S,N-CDs at 372 nm. Secondly, the emission at 465 nm of the S,N-CDs was absorbed by the peak at 446 nm of Cr(VI). So it may be IFE between S,N-CDs and Cr(VI) which causes the fluorescence quenching of S,N-CDs by Cr(VI) according to Fig. 1A, 3D and S4D. For further investigating the fluorescence quenching mechanism of S,N-CDs by Cr(VI) in detail, the fluorescence decay dynamics was performed at room temperature, and the results were shown in Fig. S4E and Tables S3. The decay curves obtained from S,N-CDs can be well-fitted using Eq. (4).
(4)
I
t
= A +
B 1
exp
−
t
τ 1
+
B 2
exp
−
t
τ 2

Here, B1 and B2 are the fluorescence intensities of the components with decay times of τ1 and τ2, respectively. According to the τ1(B1) and τ2(B1) of S,N-CDs without and with different concentration of Cr(VI), the average lifetimes were calculated in Table S3. Table S3 depicts that the average lifetimes of S,N-CDs is 7.11 ns, and the fluorescence lifetime is reduced to 5.13 ns with the addition of Cr(VI) concentration from 0 to 0.787 mmol/L, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of S,N-CDs by Cr(VI) involves a dynamic process. So we speculate that the possible quenching mechanism between S,N-CDs and Cr(VI) is the dynamic quenching and IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The nano-probe for determination of AA
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared S,N-CDs could be employed as a nano-probe system by the “on-off” process for detection of Cr(VI). Subsequently, different reducing agents (KF, KCl, KBr, Citric Acid, Glucose, KBH4, NaNO3, Na2SO3, Na2S2O3, Na2S, glutathione, Glycine, glutamic acid, Cysteine and AA) were employed to recover the fluorescence of S,N-CDs quenched by Cr(VI). Fig. 4A depicts that AA can restore the fluorescence quenched by Cr(VI). And the S,N-CDs/Cr(VI) system could be applied as an “off-on” fluorescence nano-probe for evaluation of AA. The quenched fluorescence of the S,N-CDs by Cr(VI) is restored in a moment of 2 min (Fig. S5A) with the addition of AA to the S,N-CDs/Cr(VI) solution. Fig. 4B illustrates that the fluorescence intensity of S,N-CDs progressively enhances as the concentration of AA increases. Inset of Fig. 4B enunciates that there is a good linear relationship between F0 F and AA concentration, and the linear range is observed from 6.6 to 892 μmol/L (i.e.: 1.16–157 mg/L) with a linear equation of ΔF = F - F0 = 0.3372 CAA - 4.6917 (R2 = 0.9824, n = 15), and the LOD is 76 nmol/L [19]. Subsequently, the absorption spectrum of the S,N-CDs/Cr(VI) system with the increasing of AA was examined to clarify the mechanism of fluorescence recovery. Fig. 4C illustrates that the absorbance intensity at 258 nm, 350 nm and 446 nm of Cr(VI) decreases with the increasing concentration of AA, and a new peak at 578 nm appears with a distinct isoelectric point at 507 nm (Inset of Fig. 4C). The new peak at 578 nm belongs to the Cr3+ according to Fig. S4D. So the mechanism of the fluorescence “off-on” process might be that when AA is added in the S,N-CDs/Cr(VI) solution, Cr(VI) is reduced to Cr3+ resulting in the weakening of the IFE between S,N-CDs and Cr(VI), and thereby the fluorescence of S,N-CDs is recovered. Ultimately, the number of fluorescence cycles of the prepared S,N-CDs between Cr(VI) and AA was investigated. Fig. S5B and Fig. S5C indicate that the “on-off-on” nano-probe system may have 12 cycles of Cr(VI) and AA determination, and the fluorescence intensity recovers about 73% of primitive fluorescence in every cycle. And because of weak absorption peaks of Cr3+ at 410 and 580 nm overlap partially with the excitation and emission peaks of S,N-CDs resulting in the weak IFE between Cr3+ and S,N-CDs, AA cannot completely restore the fluorescence of the as-prepared S,N-CDs quenched by Cr(VI). According to Fig. S4D, it can be calculated that the molar absorption coefficient of Cr3+ at 410 nm and Cr(VI) at 350 nm and 446 nm are 21.3, 417.9 and 2.9 × 103 L/mol/cm, respectively. The IFE of Cr(VI) and S,N-CDs is more effective than that of Cr3+ and S,N-CDs, so AA can only partially restore the fluorescence of S,N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT assays and cellular imaging of S,N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the as-prepared S,N-CDs to SMMC 7721 cells was assessed by the generally MTT assay for the further biological applications. As shown in Fig. 5A, the viability of SMMC 7721 cells keeps higher than 66% even they were incubated with the concentration of S,N-CDs at 800 μg/mL for 48 h at 37 °C, suggesting the naturally low cytotoxicity of S,N-CDs.

The above results manifest that S,N-CDs is an excellent fluorescence nano-probe and can be used for cellular identification and imaging. SMMC 7721 cells were served as model cells to investigate the intracellular detection of Cr(VI) and AA. Fig. 5B shows the bright-field (B5) and merged images (B6) of the SMMC 7721 cells incubated with 250 μg/mL S,N-CDs at 37 °C for 2 h. The cells morphology has no significant changes which confirmed the negligible cytotoxicity of S,N-CDs. Moreover, the S,N-CDs in SMMC 7721 cells could exhibit red, green, blue and yellow emissions with the excitation wavelength at 543 nm (B1), 488 nm (B2), 405 nm (B3) and 515 nm (B4), suggesting the possibility of S,N-CDs employ as a potentially multicolor bio-labeling reagent.

The imaging ability of the nano-probe for Cr(VI) and AA were surveyed in SMMC 7721 cell with the excitation wavelength at 488 nm. Fig. 6A depicts the fluorescence intensity enhances with the increase of the concentration of S,N-CDs. Fig. 6B displays that the fluorescence intensity decrease with the increasing concentration of Cr(VI), and almost completely disappeared when the concentration of Cr(VI) is 4.3 μmol/L. These results demonstrate that S,N-CDs is expected to be as an excellent nano-probe for cell imaging and Cr(VI) determination in cells. LSCM dynamic scanning was shown in Fig. 6C, when Cr(VI) entered into SMMC 7721 cells nutrient solutions, the green fluorescence is gradually weakened until almost invisible. But, when AA was added into SMMC 7721 cells nutrient solutions, the green emission was restored in 2 min (Fig. 6D), evidencing that the emission of S,N-CDs/Cr(VI) in the cells could be restored by AA. The LSCM images of SMMC 7721 cells cultured in S,N-CDs, S,N-CDs/Cr(VI), and S,N-CDs/Cr(VI)/AA (Fig. 6) retain normal morphology, which reveals that S,N-CDs, S,N-CDs/Cr(VI) and S,N-CDs/Cr(VI)/AA are biocompatible and low cytotoxicity. The above results explain that the S,N-CDs could act as an outstanding fluorescent nano-probe for “on-off-on” detection of Cr(VI) and AA in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in tap water and AA in food amples
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence nano-probe based on S,N-CDs for detection of Cr(VI) and AA in the real samples have been carried out by the proposed method. All the error analysis was based on a t-distribution with a confidence of 95% (t0.05, 4 = 2.78), as shown in Table 2 . The spike recoveries for the determination of Cr(VI) in the tap water were in the range of 94.5–101.8% with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are between 0.5–4.5% (n = 5), indicating the excellent performance of the constructed nano-probe for measuring Cr(VI) in tap-water. The potential application of the as-prepared S,N-CDs as fluorescent nano-probe for AA determination in kinds of food samples were surveyed. The results were displayed in Table 3 , the content of AA in some fresh fruits and vegetables, such as apple, strawberry, tomato, jujube, red pepper and green pepper, determined by the as-prepared “off-on” nano-probe, were 3.68, 84.28, 15.54, 244.6, 142.4 and 71.6 mg/100 g, respectively. The quantitative spike recoveries of the AA detection in these real samples were from 94.4% to 105.2%, and the RSDs were <8.8% (n = 5). All these results manifested that the as-prepared S,N-CDs maybe served as a highly selective and sensitive fluorescence nano-probe for monitoring Cr(VI) in the drinking water and AA in food samples.

In the paper, a simple and one-step acid-base neutralization and exothermic carbonization method for preparation fluorescent sulfur and nitrogen dual-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) was developed by mixing glucose, 1,2-ethylenediamine and concentrated sulfuric acid. The as-constructed S,N-CDs fluorescent nano-probe display an excellent selective, sensitive and rapid “on-off-on” response to Cr(VI) and AA. The determination of Cr(VI) is based on the IFE and dynamic process between generating Cr(VI) and S,N-CDs, and detection of AA is according to the AA weakening IFE between Cr(VI) and S,N-CDs. The fluorescence nano-probe possess many advantages, such as mild determination condition (in ultrapure water), rapid response (real-time to Cr(VI) and 2 min to AA), wide linear range (0.065–198 μmol/L (3.38–10,296 μg/L) for Cr(VI) and 6.6–892 μmol/L (1.16–157 mg/L) for AA). Furthermore, the as-constructed nano-probe was label-free used for the detection of Cr(VI) in tap water and AA in food samples successfully. Finally, MTT evaluation proved that the as-prepared S,N-CDs is low toxicity and could be used for multi-color cell imaging in SMMC 7721 cells. In view of its simple detection condition, rapid response, wide linear range, low detection limit and inexpensive instrument, the as-constructed S,N-CDs could have a wide range of potential applications, including fluorescence nano-probe, environmental water quality monitoring, food samples testing and other biological application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705101 and 21575084) and the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province, China (No.201601D102017). We acknowledge Dr. Ying Zuo and Juanjuan Wang from the Scientific Instrument Center at Shanxi University for their help with AFM and LSCM measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.02.065.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly sensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots
